### JanSz — 2017-03-05T15:57:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-and-pregnenolone-help.19171/post-209665>

[http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/topics/progesterone-therapy](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/topics/progesterone-therapy)

**Progesterone therapy**
**Overview**
[Progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) is women's second important and essential hormone and a partner of [estrogen](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Estrogen). Wherever [estradiol](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Estradiol) is acting in women's bodies (bone, brain, breasts, [uterus](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Uterus), skin and everywhere), [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) is also acting. Women have two reproductive [hormones](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Hormones)—[estrogen](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Estrogen) and [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone). Men only have one ([testosterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Testosterone)).

[Progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) therapy means taking [natural progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Natural_progesterone) ([oral micronized progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Oral_micronized_progesterone)) that is [bio-identical](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Bio-identical). If, for cost reasons, a [progestin](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progestin) must be substituted, [medroxyprogesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Medroxyprogesterone) is the closest in action to [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) (and, like [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone), also improves [hot flush](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Hot_flush)es and increases bone density). [Progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) must be given at bedtime since its major "side effect" (smile) is to improve sleep; it is effective in a dose of 300 mg at bedtime daily which keeps the serum [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) at or above the [luteal phase](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Luteal_phase) level for a full 24-hour day.

[Cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone), for menstruating women of any age, means [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) for the last two weeks of a [menstrual cycle](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Menstrual_cycle) or of a month. Based on a randomized controlled trial, this treatment with [cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) increases spinal bone density and provides regular flow for women who have reversible (usually stress-related) reasons their periods have stopped or are far apart. [Cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) is also an essential therapy for [premenopausal](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Premenopausal) women with [anovulatory androgen excess](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Anovulatory_Androgen_Excess) ([AAE](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#AAE))(also called [polycystic ovary syndrome](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Polycystic_Ovary_Syndrome) \[[PCOS](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#PCOS)\]) because it prevents [endometrial cancer](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Endometrial_Cancer), slows the [pulse](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Pulse) frequency of [luteinizing hormone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Luteinizing_hormone) ([LH](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#LH)) thus decreasing [testosterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Testosterone) production, provides regular menstrual-type flow and blocks the formation of the skin hormone that causes pimples and unwanted face hair. [Cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) is also a safe and effective treatment of [perimenopausal](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Perimenopause) cyclic [night sweats](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Night_Sweats) (that occur around the time of flow for women whose cycles are still regular). In [perimenopause](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Perimenopause), [cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) may also (especially if [ibuprofen](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Ibuprofen) is also taken) help decrease heavy flow. [Cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) also helps with the sleep and [premenstrual symptoms](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Premenstrual_symptoms) in [perimenopause](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Perimenopause).

[Early menopause](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Primary_Ovarian_Insufficiency_POI) (before age 40) can be treated with [estradiol](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Estradiol) and cyclic or daily [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone). This treatment is continued until a woman becomes age 50-52. With [cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) those women young enough to want regular flow will achieve it when [estrogen](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Estrogen) is given in a long cycle (from the first to the 25th of the month) and [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) from the 14 to the 27th. (Note—this is the only time ovarian hormone therapy is truly "replacement.")

[Progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) (taken daily) is effective treatment of [menopausal](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Menopausal) [hot flush](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Hot_flush)es as shown by a CeMCOR randomized trial. Daily [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) also significantly improves sleep in [menopausal](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Menopausal) women (based on three controlled trials). In short, [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) is effective therapy for women's [menstrual cycle](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Menstrual_cycle) disturbances or [perimenopause](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Perimenopause) and [menopause](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Menopause)\-related problems.

\- See more at: [http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/topics/progesterone-therapy#sthash.6M5ROEpG.dpuf](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/topics/progesterone-therapy#sthash.6M5ROEpG.dpuf)
\================================
\================================

[http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/topics/cyclic-progesterone-therapy](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/topics/cyclic-progesterone-therapy)

**Cyclic Progesterone Therapy**
**Overview**
"[Cyclic Progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone)" means treatment with the [natural progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Natural_progesterone) hormone ([oral micronized progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Oral_micronized_progesterone), [Prometrium®](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Prometrium) or compounded in oil) in a way that mimics the normal pattern (14 days in the last half of the [menstrual cycle](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Menstrual_cycle)) and amount (300 mg at bedtime) in the [menstrual cycle](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Menstrual_cycle). That definition may suggest this therapy would often be prescribed. But, currently, it rarely is.

[Cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) has many potential uses. In general terms, taking [cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) is a way to prevent present or future problems ([http://www.cemcor.ca/resources/preventive-powers-ovulation-and-progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ca/resources/preventive-powers-ovulation-and-progesterone)) in [premenopausal](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Premenopausal) women who are otherwise healthy but for various reasons are not ovulating and making enough [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone). These women may have hypothalamic and reversible reasons for missing, too long or irregular [menstrual cycle](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Menstrual_cycle)s. These [menstrual cycle](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Menstrual_cycle) problems are fairly rare, most common in younger women and caused by a still "growing-up" reproductive system, plus normal life stresses and feeling alone, unloved or abused, losing weight, being ill or a combination of these. Rarely these cycle changes may be caused by some of the above stressors combined with over-activity.

More common problems for which [cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) is good therapy are called "ovulatory disturbances," including [anovulation](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Anovulation) and short or insufficient [luteal phase](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Luteal_phase)s. These ovulatory disturbances can occur within "funny cycles" that are far apart or irregular, but most often are silent or "[subclinical](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Subclinical)" because they occur within perfectly regular, normal-length cycles.

[Cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) is good therapy for "silent [anovulation](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Anovulation)" which describes a normal [menstrual cycle](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Menstrual_cycle) having enough [estrogen](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Estrogen) but without regular [ovulation](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Ovulation), egg release or [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) production. An even more frequent occurrence is a silent [short luteal phase](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Short_luteal_phase) or silent insufficient [luteal phase](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Luteal_phase) in which [ovulation](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Ovulation) is present but [progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Progesterone) is high for fewer than normal days or is made for long enough but in too little amounts. Currently mild high-side-of-the-breast soreness (without whole breast soreness) before flow or a monitored cycle by [quantitative basal temperature](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Quantitative_basal_temperature) are the only ways women can reliably tell if they [ovulated](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Ovulated).

Rare but important reasons for treatment with [cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) include [anovulatory androgen excess](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Anovulatory_Androgen_Excess) (also called [PCOS](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#PCOS)), for improving fertility in cycles with short or insufficient [luteal phase](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Luteal_phase)s, or to prevent the rare kind of seizures that usually occur just before menstrual flow (catamenial epilepsy). Women in very early [perimenopause](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Perimenopause) may take [cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) for [night sweats](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Night_Sweats) that cluster around flow, for premenstrual breast tenderness or fluid retention and sleep problems. Women in [perimenopause](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Perimenopause) using [cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) to treat prolonged heavy menstrual flow or [anemia](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Anemia) often need to take in a "long cycle" (for 21 days) or daily for three months.

Currently [cyclic progesterone](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Cyclic_progesterone) is not officially indicated for any of these [physiology](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/glossary#Physiology)\-based reasons.

\- See more at: [http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/topics/cyclic-progesterone-therapy#sthash.Nlgb7H0x.dpuf](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/topics/cyclic-progesterone-therapy#sthash.Nlgb7H0x.dpuf)

\======================================================
\======================================================

[http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/cyclic-progesterone-therapy](http://www.cemcor.ubc.ca/resources/cyclic-progesterone-therapy)

**Cyclic Progesterone Therapy**
Why Might I Need To Take Cyclic Progesterone Therapy?

Progesterone is one of two important women's hormones; estrogen is the one we usually hear about. When menstrual cycle length is irregular or cycles are long or skipped, or when egg release is absent despite regular cycles, progesterone levels are low or missing.

Your doctor may prescribe progesterone or a synthetic called medroxyprogesterone (MPA), to control heavy flow, prevent irregular periods, acne, unwanted hair, or treat low bone density, or for perimenopausal sore breasts, sleep problems, heavy flow or night sweats. Cyclic progesterone therapy (not MPA) also helps women achieve pregnancy.
What Medications Can I Choose?

Oral micronized progesterone (Prometrium® or the same hormone "compounded" in oil by a pharmacist) is identical to your own hormone (or bio-identical). Because it may cause sleepiness, only take this medication on your way to bed. Three 100 mg. capsules is the dose that keeps progesterone levels in the normal range for 24 hours—it costs \*$5.50 a day.

Medroxyprogesterone (MPA) is synthetic or progestin that has been used for over 40 years. As a pill it does not provide contraception like DepoMPA, and doesn't have the same side effects while on it or difficulties when stopping it. It is stronger than progesterone so the dose that creates a progesterone-like effect is 10 mg. a day. A one-day dose costs about \*Canadian $0.36.
When Do I Take It?

The first day of menstruation is called "day 1." If you get a period regularly and your cycles are usually 27-30+ days long, start progesterone on the 14th day after flow began and take it for 14 days or until cycle day 27. If your cycles are regular but shorter (for example, if your period starts every 21–26 days), then start cyclic progesterone/MPA on cycle day 12 and continue it for 14 days or until cycle day 25.
DIAGRAM 1
[View attachment 3931](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/3931/)

Diagram 1

If your period starts before you have finished the 14 days of progesterone/MPA, finish the full 14 days, but start the next progesterone 14 days after the flow began. This allows for a "catch up" with your own (likely high) estrogen levels.
DIAGRAM 2

Diagram 2

If your period starts while you are still taking progesterone/MPA, always take it for the full 14 days.

If this early flow persists, or you are in perimenopause, then you either need daily progesterone/MPA for three months or to increase the dose of cyclic therapy to 400 mg progesterone or 20 mg MPA. The early flow is a sign that your body is making high levels of estrogen that are over-stimulating the endometrium (uterus lining) and causing heavy bleeding.

If you have not started to flow within two weeks of taking cyclic progesterone/MPA, it means your own estrogen levels are low. Stop progesterone for 14 days. After 14-days "off," start the next progesterone cycle.

As soon as your flow returns, then start taking progesterone/MPA again, 14 days after the start of your flow, as shown in Diagram 1.
Are There Any Side Effects?

There are no serious negative effects from progesterone therapy—the most important is improved sleep! It does not cause blood clots, migraine headaches or increase the risk for breast cancer. In a dose of 300 mg for 14 days, it effectively prevents endometrial cancer. Pharmacy references say that progesterone causes everything shown on "the Pill" (combined hormonal contraceptives) which contain 4 times normal estrogen doses plus synthetic progestins. Prometrium® is in peanut oil; therefore those with peanut allergy must NOT take it. With MPA the major concerns are that it increases breast cancer risk in menopausal women taking estrogen therapy and should be avoided if possible when seeking pregnancy.

You may notice some changes in your breasts, feelings of warmth and other evidences of normal progesterone action. If you get moody, feel bloated and have very sore breasts it means progesterone is temporarily stimulating your body to make higher estrogen levels. This improves after one cycle.

Download this article in PDF format You will need the free Adobe Acrobat Reader in order to read and print a PDF version of this handout. If you don't already have the program, you can download it for free here.

Dr. Jerilynn C. Prior, Scientific Director, Centre for Menstrual Cycle and Ovulation Research

by
Rob Hamilton, MD

**When IF, intermittent fasting, stop eating 4-6 hrs before bed time.**

### JanSz — 2017-03-05T17:19:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-and-pregnenolone-help.19171/post-209680>

> [Neophyte79 said:](/goto/post?id=209676)
>
> Yes, there is a vitamin cure. But we feel that the effect is not enough. The same tired than two months ago. If you can, please help pregnenolone dose.

I am not a doctor.
I am 77 year old man.
I had very low cortisol(AM) and low cortisol at other times.
I had good results using
Nutricology Pregnenolone 150 Mg Sustained Release Tablets,
[https://www.amazon.com/Nutricology-Pregnenolone-Sustained-Release-Tablets/dp/B000KII7CU/ref=sr\_1\_1\_s\_it?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1488725671&sr=1-1&keywords=pregnenolone 150mg](https://www.amazon.com/Nutricology-Pregnenolone-Sustained-Release-Tablets/dp/B000KII7CU/ref=sr_1_1_s_it?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1488725671&sr=1-1&keywords=pregnenolone+150mg)

I used it for about two years **(variable doses)**, now do not have to and my cortisol stays ok.
I used it in the morning at wakeup time. (Right when I get off the bed).
I would do rather often tests of my cortisol,serum to adjust dose.
I used dose that would give me at 7;30AM
cortisol,serum=~20ug/dL (or only slightly less)
on laboratory range=(6.2-19.4)ug/dL
I wake up 6AM.

**It is important to have steady daily schedule and good exposure to light,
that reinforces good Circadian Rhythm cycling.**

The above resulted in my somewhat elevated progesterone.
\---------------
Above should be good for
man and postmenopausal woman

Menstruating woman should have no problem using this system in first part of cycle (and large part of second half) (but may have to stop using it for a while to allow menstrual cycle to continue)(to allow sufficient progesterone fall).

Healthy man and healthy menstruating woman:
man have progesterone=1.4 ng/mL
woman have the same progesterone level in first half of cycle.
In second half of menstruating cycle healthy woman have very high progesterone.
When progesterone starts falling, when it reaches level near 1.4ng/ml bleeding starts.
And the cycle starts all over again.
So,
if menstruating woman would use pregnenolone (or progesterone) supplementation,
she must manage it in such a way that would allow menstrual cycle to continue.
So,
IF supplementation with pregnenolone or progesterone raises serum progesterone to level higher than 1.4ng/ml she must stop using it for so many days to allow for progesterone drop.
\--
Both of you (boy & girl) have low cortisol,
and are attempting to raise it using pregnenolone (or progesterone).

**This procedure, to be successful must be guided by blood tests.**

.....
Above is just a one part of overall health maintenance.

......

> Should I use DHEA, pregnenolone or just? ask for help from you in the future?

Most people are low on DHEAs.
So most should use it.
Usually 50mg/day is first dose that many can use without testing.
How much to use should be guided by tests.
DUTCH test, I posted above is good test to use.

Excess of DHEA supplementation can mess up other hormones.
Specially, androstenedione and estrone E1.

Usually recommended DHEAs levels on blood tests are:

Men 350-500 μg/dL
Women 275-400 μg/dL

....
Note that good DHEAs levels can prevent
pregnenolonme steal.
so
You may want to optimize DHEAs levels first (say up to the lower recommended level).
That
may improve
pregnenolone
progesterone
cortisolAM

If you lucky, that may be a big part of what you need.
Add lots of light (at the right time), and that may be it.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-03-06T16:33:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-and-pregnenolone-help.19171/post-209748>

Progesterone acts like a "competitive allosteric inhibitor" of aromatase levels in fat mass and is one proxy I use that tells me how much CO2 and water a mitochondria are capable of making. So this makes it a very interesting for adiocyte redox hacks. This give me a good insight into heteroplasmy rates of my mitochondria as well. I use this in a lot of my light mito-hacks. Very interesting and reliable marker in my hacks. I want tp know the rules by which I'm actually playing. Not the rules society thinks we're playing with.

We are build of cells.
Space is divided
outside cells
inside cells

nothing happens when FreeT3 is outside cells

When we swallow thyroid hormone pills,
the thyroid hormone is inside our body but outside of cells.

If there is not enough FreeCortisol that thyroid hormone will stay outside cells.

because
if you raise FreeT3 first it will pool and stay there (waiting for cortisol)
when FreeT3 is not used right away its level may raise
so
when you latter
raise cortisol level

@[Barbara Pollard](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19827/) ,

# Thread: hacking Infrared sauna
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-infrared-sauna.19173/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eilee — 2017-03-05T21:44:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-infrared-sauna.19173/post-209708>

I have a Sunlighten Mpulse full spectrum sauna. I was thinking of adding in a couple of 250W heat lamps to maximize NIR.

However, I just listened to a podcast by Leanne Venier on Extreme Health radio. She says the heat lamps are mostly ineffective for NIR, in that 95% of the bulbs are Mid/ Far IR. She also said because they're so hot, you can't really get close enough to absorb the photons. Didn't feel that for the tiny amount of Red/NIR you might get, that it was worth it. You'd have to spend like 5 hours in front of them to get the benefits of only 5-10 minutes in front of NIR LED's.

I also plan to be using a JOOV combo light (red/NIR LED), once it arrives. (I am hoping to address systemic inflammation).

Wondering if it makes sense to retrofit the sauna the the 250 W heat lamps or to simply stick with the JOOV?

### Sean L. — 2017-03-05T23:22:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-infrared-sauna.19173/post-209716>

Eilee, heat lamps were my starting point but i've since shifted to 660 nm LED grow lights and outfitted the inside with gold foil after hearing Jack talk about how this reflects the IR rays. Keeps the sauna super hot and a noticeable improvement in effectiveness. Also added black lights to get some UV.

Let me know what you end up doing. Always open to new ideas on maximizing sauna use.

Sean

### Jack Kruse — 2017-03-06T03:49:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-infrared-sauna.19173/post-209724>

### Sean L. — 2017-07-26T21:25:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-infrared-sauna.19173/post-220147>

Different light frequencies have unique effects on the body. If you are getting plenty of AM sun, you probably don't need to augment with artificial lighting. I use the sauna primarily in the winter when sunlight is low or whenever I want the performance and longevity benefits of heat shock proteins [https://tim.blog/2014/04/10/saunas-hyperthermic-conditioning-2/](https://tim.blog/2014/04/10/saunas-hyperthermic-conditioning-2/)

And yes, ideally using these lights to create a more natural and balanced light spectrum.

Some benefits to explore:

IR-A light is absorbed by cytochrome c in the mitochondria and stimulates it to produce ATP. [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27857496](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27857496)

IR-A and UVA are present in AM sun and are key to building dopamine and melatonin via the eye. [https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/time-rethink-your-truth-sun-jack-kruse](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/time-rethink-your-truth-sun-jack-kruse)

IR light builds EZ water which essentially turns your cell water into a battery and increases the amount of charge it can hold. [https://www.jackkruse.com/ubiquitination-8-the-mammalian-battery/](https://www.jackkruse.com/ubiquitination-8-the-mammalian-battery/)

UVA causes Nitric oxide to be released from the skin which dilates blood vessels, lowers blood pressure and decreases cardiac risk. [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...-you-are-coupled-to-cold-and-not-light.17029/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/do-you-need-am-uva-sunlight-in-winter-if-you-are-coupled-to-cold-and-not-light.17029/)

### Rubicon — 2017-10-05T04:07:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hacking-infrared-sauna.19173/post-225034>

# Thread: Is PPG Efficacious for Sleep Monitoring?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-ppg-efficacious-for-sleep-monitoring.19174/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mebstyne — 2017-03-05T23:39:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-ppg-efficacious-for-sleep-monitoring.19174/post-209717>

After reading John Ott's "Health and Light" (Great recommendation Jack! Thank you.), I found myself thinking at the world from a different perspective. The story about the green filtered light being associated with the classroom of children with the highest rates of leukemia in the country stuck with me. Now, it's important to remember that Ott was not claiming that the green light was causal. But, he did not the association.

Meanwhile, each night, I tend to strap on a device to my wrist to monitor my sleep. And, this device uses Photoplethysmography (PPG) for monitoring sleep. Every minute it wakes up and flashes two bright (light up the room bright) green LEDs against my skin at my wrist and takes a reading.

Now, there are three question I want to raise:

1) "**Is monitoring sleep via bright green LEDs strapped to the wrist a good idea from a health perspective?"** First, I want to thank you all, for challenging me and helping me grow. I would never have asked this question, if I hadn't been researching light, EMF, and other aspects of environment that impact health. Now, I'd love help reigning in research-based and leading theory-based thinking on what the answer to that question may be.

2) Simplifying assumption #1: Assume for a moment that my PPG device used blue LEDs on my wrist. (Let's ignore that they are green for a moment.) Keep in mind, for 8 hours of sleep, the LEDs are "on" for about 2.6 hours (turning on about 20 seconds every minute). Again, they are right against my skin. Clearly, we know from devices like the re-timer that blue LEDs in the nose or ears can have a physiological effect. How about the wrist? How about this 20 second blast every 8 hours for 2.6 hours through the night? What stress might this induce? How severe is that stress, in terms of other health conditions, measurable biomarkers, in terms of studies done, etc.?

3) Simplifying assumption #2: Assume for a moment that instead of a wrist device with 2 LEDs, I had an incandescent green light that flashed on my entire body every minute for 20 seconds (about 2.6 hours each night). What stress might this pose to the system? How might we characterize the severity of that stress in terms of measurable studies, etc.?

Just food for thought. Comments welcomed.

### OptimalSkr — 2017-03-06T01:26:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-ppg-efficacious-for-sleep-monitoring.19174/post-209719>

The best sleep hack is to have absolute darkness. I don't think the information about your sleep could be as beneficial as just not using it. It seems like it will screw up circadian timing.

### Sue-UK — 2017-03-06T13:03:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-ppg-efficacious-for-sleep-monitoring.19174/post-209738>

> [mebstyne said:](/goto/post?id=209717)
>
> After reading John Ott's "Health and Light" (Great recommendation Jack! Thank you.), I found myself thinking at the world from a different perspective. The story about the green filtered light being associated with the classroom of children with the highest rates of leukemia in the country stuck with me. Now, it's important to remember that Ott was not claiming that the green light was causal. But, he did not the association.
>
> Meanwhile, each night, I tend to strap on a device to my wrist to monitor my sleep. And, this device uses Photoplethysmography (PPG) for monitoring sleep. Every minute it wakes up and flashes two bright (light up the room bright) green LEDs against my skin at my wrist and takes a reading.
>
> Now, there are three question I want to raise:
>
> 1) "**Is monitoring sleep via bright green LEDs strapped to the wrist a good idea from a health perspective?"** First, I want to thank you all, for challenging me and helping me grow. I would never have asked this question, if I hadn't been researching light, EMF, and other aspects of environment that impact health. Now, I'd love help reigning in research-based and leading theory-based thinking on what the answer to that question may be.
>
> 2) Simplifying assumption #1: Assume for a moment that my PPG device used blue LEDs on my wrist. (Let's ignore that they are green for a moment.) Keep in mind, for 8 hours of sleep, the LEDs are "on" for about 2.6 hours (turning on about 20 seconds every minute). Again, they are right against my skin. Clearly, we know from devices like the re-timer that blue LEDs in the nose or ears can have a physiological effect. How about the wrist? How about this 20 second blast every 8 hours for 2.6 hours through the night? What stress might this induce? How severe is that stress, in terms of other health conditions, measurable biomarkers, in terms of studies done, etc.?
>
> 3) Simplifying assumption #2: Assume for a moment that instead of a wrist device with 2 LEDs, I had an incandescent green light that flashed on my entire body every minute for 20 seconds (about 2.6 hours each night). What stress might this pose to the system? How might we characterize the severity of that stress in terms of measurable studies, etc.?
>
> Just food for thought. Comments welcomed.

With the veins and arteries being close to the surface, the wrist can be a “window” of opportunity (like the Quantlet), or a window to chaos, (chronic exposure to pulsed or continuous unopposed single or narrow band frequencies with no “balancing” frequencies that would occur in natural light). In context, it might be beneficial to have a short duration LED treatment of a particular frequency as a systemic “tonic” dose of light into the bloodstream through the nose, ears or wrists, taking into account its chronobiological significance, but I think that the same treatment used chronically, at the wrong dose, and/or at the wrong timing, can turn it into a poison.

This article is about wound healing using green LED’s at 518nm. [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22380691](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22380691). So in principle and in its context, right dose and duration, potential benefits. Directed at wound, local and secondary systemic effects, but when the wound heals, no further treatment so no chronic systemic exposure. But what would happen systemically if after wound healing, the treatments were continued chronically? What drew my attention was the mention of it raising VEGF, and the wiki entry for VEGF said “_When VEGF is overexpressed, it can contribute to disease. Solid cancers cannot grow beyond a limited size without an adequate blood supply; cancers that can express VEGF are able to grow and metastasize. Overexpression of VEGF can cause vascular disease in the retina of the eye and other parts of the body”.
_
So apart from circadian mismatches, the effects of pulsing, nnemf etc, from that one study, indicating green as pro growth, I think that having that amount of any unopposed green frequency pulsed into my wrist chronically at night is not a risk I’d be willing to take.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Biohacking in the jungle!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-in-the-jungle.19175/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matt Wilson — 2017-03-06T04:52:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-in-the-jungle.19175/post-209730>

Hola! I’m Matt Wilson, co-founder of the travel company Under30Experiences and host of the Live Different Podcast. I moved here in Costa Rica, to eat fresh fish, live in the jungle away from most EMF, and have my feet grounded in the ocean everyday.

I’m an athletic 31, 5’10” and 150 lbs and need some help deciphering my WellnessFX Comprehensive labs… I’ve done lots of Dr. Kruse’s webinars and was sponsored by Bulletproof, so this is not a newbie ask.

My lifestyle is pretty damn strict, but I am on the computer 5+ hours per day (grounded) and have 15 employees, so there is some stress…. I eat very keto; eggs, fish, avocados, and sauerkraut at most meals. I strength train 3x/week, meditate 30-60min daily, drink R/O, tan my @$$ at 7am every morning, wear my blueblockers, and haven't taken a warm shower in over a year...

I’m a concerned about my Thyroid and hormone panel… T3, Free T3, and IGF1 are a little low, although Testosterone comes in at 1191 ng/dl, Androgen at 520 ug/dL, and SHBG at 60 nmo/L (all high!) Also notable when talking about Thyroid is my Total Iron Binding Capacity is 229 ug/dL (low).

My theory is that low body fat (less than 8%), combined with intermittent fasting, long hours, and lots of travel, has pointed me in the direction of hypothyroidism. This was deduced from listening to Ben Greenfield + Elle Russ’s Paleo Thyroid Solution, but maybe I’m wrong…

I know this drawing of blood is just a snapshot of this a moment in my life, but any insights on this would be appreciated. Gracias + Pura vida!

### Matt Wilson — 2017-03-08T14:00:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-in-the-jungle.19175/post-209862>

Moving this to the thread to the labs section! Thanks all.

### NADme — 2017-05-14T13:19:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-in-the-jungle.19175/post-214025>

Your extensive panel, is that a single comprehensive CPT that Quest offers or did you menu the composite?

Your SHBG is high and while total T is ranged for your age your free T might be better served by addressing your SHBG.

You do not have E2/estradiol on your list. Quest's E2 is not good, Labcorp sensitive:

**Estradiol, Sensitive, LC/MS**
TEST: 140244
CPT: 82670

Also, you need a urine iodine and selenium RBC, I use labcorp for those as well. You cant supplement one without the other or you will imbalance your thyroid. Dont eat too many Brasil nuts which has, if memory serves, 20-80 micrograms of selenium per nut. I was able to reduce thickening and eliminate 1-2 small nodules. Dont use lugals for iodine, i use detoxadine, nascent iodine.

E2, Iodine and Selenium will give you a better picture. Your SHBG at 60 is an issue.

Are you out by the University for Peace in the Jungle? I  CR. Lots of pals in the land of Tico/Tica. Exchange was 120:1 when I fist traveled to CR. Ironic the guy who is the big shot at the University for Peace was the co-author of Agenda 29 and advocate of eugenics.

# Thread: Cutting my hair? WHY?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cutting-my-hair-why.19176/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Nicolaj Sølvsten — 2017-03-06T11:57:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cutting-my-hair-why.19176/post-209737>

What is the function of hair? Is it only to keep us warm, I think not. Why do humans grow so much hair on their heads? I am thinking it is tied to the mitochondrial density of the brain. I read this [https://www.quantumbalancing.com/spiritualhair.htm](https://www.quantumbalancing.com/spiritualhair.htm), about a year ago and decided to never cut my hair again (all hair), I have lived by that decision for 1 and 1/2 years. Is there a quantum explanation as to the importance of hair for human health?

To me it is clear that other animals do not cut their hair.. obviously... so human should't either I assume.
What I am curious about however is why we have hair looking from a quantum perspective.

The above mentioned article describes how if hair is left un-cut for about 3 years, the hair will act as antennae for cosmic energy, I am thinking sun-light. Here is an excerpt from the article:

_“When the hair on your head is allowed to attain its full, mature length, then phosphorous, calcium, and vitamin D are all produced, and enter the lymphatic fluid, and eventually the spinal fluid through the two ducts on the top of the brain. This ionic change creates more efficient memory and leads to greater physical energy, improved stamina, and patience.”

Yogi Bhajan explained that if you choose to cut your hair, you not only lose this extra energy and nourishment, but your body must then provide a great amount of vital energy and nutrients to continually re-grow the missing hair.

In addition, hairs are the antennas that gather and channel the sun energy or prana to the frontal lobes, the part of the brain you use for meditation and visualization. These antennas act as conduits to bring you greater quantities of subtle, cosmic energy. It takes approximately three years from the last time your hair was cut for new antennas to form at the tips of the hair._

### Andrea — 2017-03-06T16:47:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/twitching-while-sleeping.19178/post-209752>

1.
Why do some twitch during sleep? In different muscles, throughout the whole body. Twitches can come a couple minutes apart, sometimes several times a minute.

2.
Once when I was close to someone while they were sleeping and I was wide awake, I got the idea to send them some warm good energy. I had my hand on their chest, making sure my fingertips too touched their skin. I breathed in deep, imagining getting filled with energy, and as I breathed out I imagined loving, warm energy flowing through my arm and fingertips into their heart. They twitched in their upper body. So I tried it again. They twitched again. And again.

What's going on?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-40-dealing-with-depression-and-brain-fog-and-fatigue.19179/post-209787>

I am 40 years old women with facing a lot of health issues . I am experiencing very low immunity and fall sick very often .
I have gained 50 lb in 3 years . I am undergoing with lot of depression and brain fog . I take wellbutrin(200 mg) and Lexapro(10 mg) since 3 years .
my health issue as below . I feel there is something going on with my gut and inflammation .

1.  Depression and anxiety and feel very flat after antidepressent
2.  Severe brain fog and doesnt feel like moving the needle.
3.  Migrane
4.  Fluctuating bp since 2 weeks very high .
5.  Anemic . I recently had to take 2 unit blood since my hemoglobin was 7
6.  I experience very bloating .
7.  High thyroid and take Levothyroxine 100 mg
8.  Dont feel fresh at all after 10 hours sleep

My blood panel result is this

TSH 4.30 uIU/mL

Total cholesterol 206 mg/dL
Triglyceride 102 mg/dL
HDL cholesterol 50 mg/dL
LDL Calculated 136 mg/dL
C-Reactive Protein 9.0 mg/L
Ferritin 9 ng/mL
White Blood Cell Count 4.3 K/uL
Red Blood Cell Count 4.45 M/uL
Hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL
Hematocrit 34.1 %
MCV 77 fL
MCH 24.9 pg
MCHC 32.6 g/dL
RDW 15.1 %
Platelet Count 203 K/uL
Iron 63 ug/dL
Iron Binding 407 ug/dL
Iron % Saturation 15 %

Please help me with the following

1.  Since my cholesterol and Triglyceride is very high , is it ok to take Paleo ?
2.  What kind of routine and supplement I should follow .
3.  My doctor want to tapper my antidepressent and what kind of routine can help me to get out from depression ?
4.  What test I can do to know what is my issue with gut of know my inflamation ?
5.  How do I test what issue going on with my gut
6.  Where should I do my lab?

Here is my goal

1.  Treat my depression and get out from antidepressent
2.  Improve my mental performance
3.  Improve my physique (I'm ~5'3'' and ~176lbs so I'm not trying to lose weight, to 145 lb.)
4.  Cure my gut and inflamation
5.  Controll BP
6.  Get rid of severe brain fog

    Here is a breakdown of the bio-hacks I've been working on:
    1.  Diet. Pretty straight-forward as this is probably the most important bio-hack of all.Eating Paleo .

Please suggest if I can do Paleo diet?

1.  Exercise \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\* I started doing HIIT 20 min everyday
2.  Mediation mindfulness . I recently started mindfulness meditation 4 day a week.
3.  Oil Pulling. I've been swishing around coconut oil in my mouth for 20 minutes/2 day week
4.  Powerful posing. I recently watched a Ted talk by Amy Cuddy on this. I've been trying to do these powerful poses every day multiple times a day in the hopes that I will fake it till I become a more self-confident, risk-taking person able to take on any challenge.

### JanSz — 2017-03-08T23:15:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-40-dealing-with-depression-and-brain-fog-and-fatigue.19179/post-209920>

[http://hillbillyhannah.blogspot.com/2012/08/answers-mthfr-and-pai-1.html](http://hillbillyhannah.blogspot.com/2012/08/answers-mthfr-and-pai-1.html)

**Sunday, August 26, 2012**
**Answers - MTHFR and PAI-1**

Well, we finally have some answers about what likely happened to Jacob. My recent bloodwork revealed I have two blood clotting disorders. I am heterozygous for MTHFR and **PAI-1 4g/4g**. These are caused by genetic mutations, and heterozygous means I have one copy of each muation which means I inherited one copy from either my mom or dad. I know it's kinda confusing. Both of these are definitely associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes, such as reoccurent misccariage, stillbirth, placental abruption, pre-eclampsia, etc.

While there were no obvious clots in the umblical cord or my placenta, these conditions can cause microclots that aren't necessarily seen by visual inspection. This likely explains the small areas of blood loss on my placenta From what I understand, the MTHFR makes me more prone to clots, and the PAI means I don't break clots down well once they form. There is no cure for either one, but they are treatable. In a subsequent pregnancy, I will take baby asprin and most likely lovenox injections, which is a blood thinner similar to heperin. I read somewhere that the lovenox does not cross the placenta but allows proper bloodflow to the placenta. The asprin, apparently, does cross the placenta and keeps blood clots from forming in the baby. I will also need extra folate and specific B vitamins to treat the MTHFR. I will be on this and possibly even baby asprin for the rest of my life. Here is more info about MTHFR. It probably does a better job explaining it than I can.

\*\*\*\*\*\*

What is MTHFR?

MTHFR - (Methylene-tetra-hydro-folate-reductase) is an enzyme found in the cells of our body. It is needed to metabolize (break down) homocysteine, an amino acid found in the proteins you eat. Elevated homocysteine levels have been associated with fetal neural tube defects (i.e., spinal Bifida) and miscarriage. An elevated homocysteine level can also indicate a increased risk factor for blood clots, arteriosclerosis (hardening of arteries) and strokes in both men and women. The metabolism of homocysteine depends on several enzymes, one of which is MTHFR, along with B vitamins and folic acid. In other words, B vitamins, folic acid and MTHFR are necessary to keep homocysteine levels within normal limits.

Sometimes your MTHFR has a genetic defect. These defects are fairly common and its estimated by some studies that up to 60% of the population have one MTHFR mutation or another.

There are two common genetic MTHFR mutations. If you have one mutation only, you are heterozygous. It is reported that nearly half the population is MTHFR heterozygous. - it is nothing abnormal. If you have two copies of the same mutation, you are homozygous. Homozygotes can have elevated homocysteine levels in maternal blood and amniotic fluid. If you have one copy each of the two different mutations, you are compound heterozygotes. Compound heterozygotes have the same treatment and risk level as homozygotes. If you have neither mutation, you are negative and don't need to worry about MTHFR.

There is NO CURE for MTHFR mutation. If you have the mutation, that's the way your body is made. You can, however, lessen any risk factors you have by taking B vitamins, Folic acid and baby aspirin in doses prescribed by your doctor. By taking these agents, you can lower your homocysteine levels and therefor your risk for the conditions above.

MTHFR mutations are hereditary. Families with significant early cardiovascular disease or recurrent pregnancy loss might benefit from clinical investigation.

\*\*\*\*\*\*\*

I"ve learned the MTHFR is also linked to many other health issues not related to pregnancy. So, if you're a nerd like me and want to read more about it this website has alot of info. [http://mthfr.net/](http://mthfr.net/). There are specific guidlines on the proper forms of folate and B Vitamins, people with mtfhr need. They are not created equal.

As for the PAI, I've had a hard time finding good information on it in layman's terms. The information below is the easiest to understand that I've come across, and it's still not a leisurely read

\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*

**First of all, what is PAI-1?** The best way to explain PAI-1 is to describe what it does. The blood clotting system is made up of many different factors, some which cause a clot to form and others which cause the clot to breakdown, and these coexist in a very delicate balance. When the blood clotting pathways are activated, fibrinogen, a soluble plasma glycoprotein, is polymerized to form fibrin which is then cross-linked by the action of factor XIII to form the ‘clot’. Under normal circumstances, as soon as a clot forms, it begins to be broken down by other plasma factors. Specifically, a substance called tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) converts an inactive plasma protein, plasminogen, to the active substance, plasmin, which then plays a critical role in the breakdown of fibrin (fibrinolysis), thereby ‘dissolving’ the clot. (Interestingly, and perhaps germane to our discussion at some later point, plasmin also plays important roles in ovulation, cell migration, and epithelial cell differentiation).

PAI-1 fits into this balance as the primary _inhibitor_ of tPA and other ‘plasminogen activators’ in the blood. To put its role in perspective, by inhibiting tPA, PAI-1 prevents the activation of plasminogen, thereby controlling the rate and extent of fibrin degradation (clot break down, or fibrinolysis) that occurs. Overactivity of PAI-1 will therefore lead to a tendency to form (or maintain) clots and an underactivity will result in an increased risk for bleeding. (With regard to other known functions of plasmin mentioned above, overactivity of PAI-1 might then also impair ovulation, cell migration, and epithelial cell differentiation).

Control of PAI-1 production is complex, but it is at least partly determined on a genetic basis. Certain polymorphisms of **PAI-1, 4G/4G** and 4G/5G, are associated with increased blood concentrations of PAI-1. Elevated PAI-1 levels have been correlated with risk for both arterial and venous thromboembolic conditions (e.g., deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, and stroke) and atherosclerotic disease (.eg., coronary and carotid artery disease), especially if other genetic (e.g., factor V Leiden; prothrombin G20210A; MTHFR polymorphisms; protein C, protein S, and antithrombin III deficiencies) or inherited (e.g., antiphospholipid antibodies; lupus anticoagulant; anti-beta-1-glycoprotein) [thrombophilias](http://www.healthline.com/blogs/pregnancy_childbirth/2007/04/recurrent-early-pregnancy-loss-7.html) are also present. Individuals with insulin resistance syndromes and diabetes mellitus frequently have elevated PAI-1 levels. Obesity and hyperlipidemia are also associated with elevated PAI-1 and under certain circumstances, weight reduction and/or reduction in cholesterol and triglycerides, can lead to reduction in PAI-1 levels.

\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*

**This website has much more infomation on PAI and how it relates to pregnancy.** Again, this isn't pleasure reading, but it is fairly understandable [http://www.healthline.com/health-bl...or-1-pai-1-role-adverse-pregnancy-outcome-2-l](http://www.healthline.com/health-blogs/fruit-womb/plasminogen-activator-inhibitor-1-pai-1-role-adverse-pregnancy-outcome-2-l).

I'm sorry this post is so full of information, but I put it up here so my family and friends will better understand and also for any other women who are effected by these issues.

Ok, so how do I feel about all this? The day I received the news was a bit overwhelming. I was thankful to have an answer, true facts to work with, but also kinda discouraged. I mean who wants to find out there is something else wrong with you. Now, that I've had about a week to think it over and to read more about these disorders I am feeling more hopeful. I've read about and heard about many women with blood clotting issues that have successful pregnancies. I try to remind myself that I was able to carry Jacob for 6 months with no added supplementation or medication, so I should be able to carry full-term the next time around. I sooooo wish I knew about all this a year ago because I know my sweet boy would still be alive if I had known....if only....if only....if only.

It probably seems like I've done alot of research, but I haven't really. I am careful about how much I focus on it. That's another reason it's taken over a week to share this. I must be wise and prepare my body the best I can for another pregnancy, but somedays being pregnant again is the farthest thing from my mind. How do you grieve one baby and prepare for another? It's tough, and it's not how it's supposed to be.

### ValerieBee — 2017-09-08T17:21:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannabis-and-cancer.19180/post-223170>

we were able to use cannabis for my kiddo to supplement his chemo. The science is sparse, but that is due to politics. If you're starting from zero as I was, I found the book Cannabis Revealed by Bonni Goldstein, MD to be a helpful primer. It covers more diseases than cancer, and breaks things down nicely.

I am also researching this as well, as we are now looking at maintenance dosing for my kiddo....pediatric cancer remission cannabis maintenance dosing is not a topic for peer review, so I'm just piecing it together as I go, and following my instincts. My instincts lead me here, so I'm doing alright

I didn't know about leptin when I started researching this, so the redox information and leptin links take the research to a new level. I just came across this paper (from 2016), which might interest you. I'd love to see this thread grow so we can help each other with this info!

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

**[Modulation of cellular redox homeostasis by the endocannabinoid system](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4852457/)**

**Abstract**
The endocannabinoid system (ECS) and reactive oxygen species (ROS) constitute two key cellular signalling systems that participate in the modulation of diverse cellular functions. Importantly, growing evidence suggests that cross-talk between these two prominent signalling systems acts to modulate functionality of the ECS as well as redox homeostasis in different cell types. Herein, we review and discuss evidence pertaining to ECS-induced regulation of ROS generating and scavenging mechanisms, as well as highlighting emerging work that supports redox modulation of ECS function. Functionally, the studies outlined reveal that interactions between the ECS and ROS signalling systems can be both stimulatory and inhibitory in nature, depending on cell stimulus, the source of ROS species and cell context. Importantly, such cross-talk may act to maintain cell function, whereas abnormalities in either system may propagate and undermine the stability of both systems, thereby contributing to various pathologies associated with their dysregulation.

> we were able to use cannabis for my kiddo to supplement his chemo. The science is sparse, but that is due to politics. If you're starting from zero as I was, I found the book Cannabis Revealed by Bonni Goldstein, MD to be a helpful primer. It covers more diseases than cancer, and breaks things down nicely.

Hi there Valerie

This is John. I am delighted to see that you have been through an adventure as well and managed to win this twisted disease. We are facing a very aggressive brain tumor with our three year old boy as outlined in [this thread](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/grade-iv-astrocytoma-on-our-3-year-old-boy.21144/). We are based in Europe (Athens / Greece) and we are looking into the potential benefits of Cannabis oil for treating / supporting our little one.

Literature is sparse indeed, but it seems that oils rich in THC (and CBD) are especially effective in treating such kind of cancers, in parallel with traditional methods (chemo / radio).

I know that things are a lot easier and open minded in the US regarding cannabis, but I am doing the best I can to find resources in Europe. [Spain](http://www.bbm1.ucm.es/cannabis/investigacion2_en.htm#Tumores_cerebrales) has some promising research going on, especially for this kind of brain tumor.

If you have any insight / information from your own research about similar resources in Europe, we would be thankful to hear about them.

Many thanks for your reply.

God Bless.
John

The endocannabinoids (ECS) are structurally similar to arachidonic acid and have been suggested to interfere with the inflammatory process. They have also been shown to inhibit cancer cell proliferation because of one key reason: All ECS slow ECT in mitochondria and all cancer cells need an intact ECT to remain cancer. When ECT is fast......Apopotosis is IMPOSSIBLE. This is what allows cancer to grow.

What builds the cannabinoid system best?

SUN.

John, I haven't been active in this forum for a few months--Life interrupted. I don't know of any UK resources off the top of my head. However, I can hook you up with the Holistic Cancer Parents page on facebook--one of the members there clued me in to Dr. Kruse's work as I was posting questions about EMFs. My kiddo is coming up on 1 year cancer free. That page was my go-to resource for info and support, and it was fantastic. We did all we could to reduce his EMF exposure, and we continue that process. Of course the docs said there was "no evidence" that EMFs could have caused his tumor, but you know there is, and this was the only logical culprit in his medical history. So don't get discouraged when docs are dismissive. My kiddo gets as much sun as he can (i'm thinking of homeschooling next year so he can get even more). He loves earthing, and can explain it pretty well for a 5 year old! You are going through such a terribly hard journey! Please feel free to message me for any kind of support I could offer. We were able to end my kiddo's chemo earlier than docs planned ("against medical advice"). I'm happy to share any parts of our journey. So glad you found this community! Sending healing thoughts for your boy and solace for your whole family. Much Love, Valerie.

My friend had skin cancer, and he felt just unreal pain and could not get rid of it. He even went through two rounds of chemotherapy, but still couldn't get rid of it ... I tried to persuade the doctor to prescribe morphine, but he was absolutely against it. In the end, he suggested trying CBD, which is often used to relax the nervous system and dull pain. The problem was that my friend absolutely could not stand this taste, and so I decided to buy all-natural [https://weedsmart.net/product-category/marijuana-flowers/bulk/](https://weedsmart.net/product-category/marijuana-flowers/bulk/) and this helped him a lot, and he stopped suffering from pain. Unfortunately, he eventually died and the doctors couldn't do anything...

### jrobertson1984 — 2021-10-04T00:28:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cannabis-and-cancer.19180/post-304537>

Numerous studies have shown that marijuana itself can be addictive if misused and overdosed. People addicted to this drug experience all the withdrawal symptoms (when they try to quit marijuana), their academic or work performance declines, and interpersonal relationships crumble just as they do with other, more "hard" drugs. If you think someone is developing (or has developed) an addiction to marijuana, you can help them understand that they have an addiction and then help them overcome it. So I use [https://www.mailorder-marijuana.com/buy-edibles-online](https://www.mailorder-marijuana.com/buy-edibles-online) and try not to overdo it, but to enjoy it.

# Thread: Screen time biohack
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-biohack.19181/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Butters — 2017-03-07T22:37:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-biohack.19181/post-209844>

Some time ago I thought using laptop during daytime isnt as bad as at night.
It seems I was dead wrong.
So despite I already use f.lux on 2700K at daytime I plan to only use the laptop in the evening hours.
Thoughts behind that are:

Pros:
\-blocking most of the fake blue light during the day
\-I already wear blueblockers in the evening so I will be protected
\-saving time during the day, because I cant drift away doing useless computer stuff in the daytime and use the daylight to the max
\-more focus on important things, because I dont just look something up on the computer

Cons:
\-when working on important things in need of the computer, attention may suffer in the evening
\-risk to stay up longer than usual, but that can easy be controlled

What do you guys say? Good idea or bad?
Or would you guys say f.lux on 2700K for light protection is enough during the day (I think I know the answer already)?

### Jack Kruse — 2017-03-08T03:20:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-biohack.19181/post-209852>

Of course this is wrong. the color temperature of the computer does not equal the sun. The spectral density is far different.

### Mitchell97 — 2017-03-09T12:07:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-biohack.19181/post-209936>

I'd leave flux on 1200k all the time or use RedScreen (I prefer it over flux) and also take the computer outside or if you use it inside use some sort of eye protection. To change it to 1200k during the day, you set flux to 1200k for night time and during the day you change the time in the flux app to the night time settings. If you're going to use it at night I recommend you download RedScreen and dim the screen a lot to not lower melatonin production.

### Butters — 2017-03-09T20:44:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-biohack.19181/post-209967>

I already use f.lux on 1200K at night + blueblockers. I will try to lower the setting at daytime. But I have Windows and it seems to be set at 2700K minimum.
I just tried RedScreen - doesnt do so much more. I already have the screen dimmed down to the minimum.

### Penny — 2017-03-09T21:32:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-biohack.19181/post-209981>

IMHO, looking at this screen for more than 10 minutes a day will make my eyes burn... even with flux set to its lowest settings plus UVEX blue blockers on - outside, not so bad, but there is a lot of glare and the net book sucks speed wise - staying off the bloody thing is the best thing - at night - just do not go on it - your skin is still absorbing the light - in a pinch I will do the "darkroom" mode on flux which is really weird, but it is still a fail - really, find something else to do and if you have to go on it, print out stuff you want to read for later - the idea is to get back to nature, not keep limping along with techno-crap...

### JanSz — 2017-03-08T17:48:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fiber-options.19183/post-209896>

This looks to me as a fiber from a wheat.
That would be the outside shell of a grain.
This days there is a problem with that outside shell.
Historically, that shell was rather soft and pliable.
Now, Monsanto re-engineered seed of wheat so that shell is hard.
It is done because it is easier to grow that kind of wheat.
When someone eats it, it is similar to sand paper, it scratches and injuries intestinal walls.

If you must eat wheat, look for heritage varieties which were not altered.

heritage grains
ancient grains
